items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: Why does correlation not imply causation in geographical data?
items.correct: Two variables may correlate because of a third confounding factor, coincidence, or reverse causation — statistical association alone does not prove one causes the other.
items.misconception: If two variables correlate strongly, one must cause the other.
items.random_wrong: Correlation and causation are the same thing.
items.why: Classic example: ice cream sales correlate with drownings — both caused by hot weather, neither causes the other. In geography this matters for evaluating claims like 'urbanisation causes obesity'.

items.q: What changed between the MDGs (Millennium Development Goals) and the SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals)?
items.correct: MDGs (2000-2015) targeted LICs on basic poverty/health/education; SDGs (2015-2030) apply to all countries and include environmental and inequality goals — broader, more universal scope.
items.misconception: The SDGs replaced the MDGs because the MDGs were achieved.
items.random_wrong: MDGs and SDGs are the same — just renamed.
items.why: Most MDGs were partly achieved but with major gaps. SDGs expanded scope to include HICs (which all face sustainability challenges) and added climate, inequality and oceans — reflecting a shift in development thinking.

items.q: What ocean temperature is needed for tropical storms?
items.why: Correct answer: 27 degrees. “20 degrees” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Saffir-Simpson. “Beaufort” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Australia 2019-20 wildfires burnt how many million hectares?
items.why: The real figure is 18 million ha. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
